_note: All examples below use the `fastReader.sh` wrapper (Linux/macOS) shorthand. Prepend the skill folder's absolute path, e.g. `~/.claude/skills/fastReader/fastReader.sh ...`. On Windows substitute `fastReader.bat`. Raw Python fallback: `PYTHONPATH=<parent-of-skill-dir> python3 -m fastReader.<cmd>` — but PYTHONPATH must be the PARENT of the fastReader folder, not the folder itself. The wrapper avoids that foot-gun.

examples: # Scan one file (prints marker-count histogram + hash; this is always your first move)
# Load many files at once - each gets its own hash, same call
# Slice-load: carve out lines START..START+COUNT-1 as a new hashed sub-document.
# Use this after toc shows you that section 4 spans 1382 lines starting at line 668.
# Load and immediately search inside (shortcut for common case)
# Recall an older Claude Code session transcript
use_cases: User says 'look at this file'             -> load <file>
User says 'search across my N files'      -> load <files...>  then search --manifests <all-hashes>
User says 'drill into section X'          -> load <file> --line <start> <count>  (from the toc you saw)
User says 'what did we talk about X'      -> load ~/.claude/projects/<slug>/<session>.jsonl  then search
User says 'quickly show me a huge file'   -> load <file>  (read the histogram, choose a scanner; do NOT Read it)

examples: # Sections with line ranges and sizes - the essential markdown overview
# Hypothesis + conclusion view: intro AND closing thought of every section, no entry cap
# JSONL record-level navigation - each line at bracket depth 1
# HTML / XML navigation by tag nesting (pick depth from the load histogram)
# Fallback for files with no detectable structure
use_cases: 'overview this doc'                 -> --sections --show-line-range-count --sample-size 80
'which sections are meatiest'       -> --sections --show-line-range-count (sort by line_span)
'gist + conclusion of each section' -> --sample-size 80 --end-sample-size 120
'list every function in this py'    -> --indent-depth 0 (top-level) or --indent-depth 1 (methods)
'what record types in this jsonl'   -> --bracket-depth 1 --sample-size 120
'map out this HTML page'            -> --tag-depth N  (pick N from the load histogram — often 5 for <div>-heavy docs)
'no headers, unstructured text'     -> --blocks --show-line-range-count

examples: # Get a whole section's content (use after toc to identify section N)
# Block (fixed-size fallback chunk, useful for files without headers)
use_cases: After toc reveals section of interest    -> get --section N     (read it in full)
Chapter-level drill                      -> get --chapter N
Subsection drill                         -> get --subsection N
No markdown headers, raw block fallback  -> get --block N
Tip: for bracket/tag/indent-depth rows, get has no flag for them — use `load --line START COUNT` to re-ingest the slice and then toc/get on the new hash.

examples: # Cross-search across many loaded files at once
# All keywords on the same line (AND) rather than any-match (OR)
use_cases: 'find X across all loaded files'    -> search X --manifests <all-hashes>
'find the exact word X, not Xy'     -> search X --manifests <hash> --exact
'find lines with both A and B'      -> search A B --manifests <hash> --all
'find where a topic is discussed'   -> search X  (hits include chapter/section/subsection breadcrumbs)
Bridge from search to get: each hit shows `section N ln <start>  <preview>` — use that N in `get --section N`.
